eli3: Stratified sampling divides the population into non-overlapping, exhaustive subgroups (strata) based on a relevant variable (age groups, regions, income levels), then independently draws a SRS from each stratum. The strata are chosen so that units within strata are more similar (homogeneous) than units across strata — this homogeneity is what reduces variance compared to SRS.

There are two main allocation rules: proportional allocation (nₕ/n = Nₕ/N — same sampling fraction in every stratum) and Neyman optimal allocation (nₕ ∝ Nₕσₕ — sample more from strata that are larger or more variable). Neyman allocation minimizes total variance for a fixed total n. If σₕ is the same across strata, Neyman reduces to proportional allocation.

Stratified sampling guarantees that each stratum is represented in the sample. This is crucial when certain subgroups are small but important (domain estimation), when stratum-specific estimates are needed, or when the relationship between X and Y differs across strata (interaction). The variance reduction vs SRS is (proportional allocation): Var_strat = Var_SRS − Σ(Nₕ/N)(μₕ−μ)²/n — the second term is the between-stratum variance removed.

whenWhy.title: Reducing estimator variance for a given n
whenWhy.body: When a stratifying variable is correlated with the outcome Y, stratification removes between-stratum variance from the estimator — reducing the variance of x̄_strat below Var_SRS at the same n. Example: income survey stratified by occupation (doctors, teachers, laborers). Income is highly variable within all workers but much less variable within each occupation — stratification captures this.

whenWhy.title: Ensuring adequate representation of small subgroups
whenWhy.body: In SRS, rare subgroups may be underrepresented (e.g., n = 5 from a 2% minority in a sample of n = 250). Oversampling minority strata (disproportionate stratification) then reweighting with the Horvitz-Thompson estimator ensures adequate precision for subgroup estimates while maintaining overall population estimates. Standard in health surveys (NHANES, NHIS), exit polls, and clinical subgroup analyses.

whenWhy.title: Administrative stratification
whenWhy.body: When sampling is conducted independently by different organizations or regions (states, hospitals, schools), stratification by organizational unit is natural. Each stratum is sampled independently, allowing stratum-level estimates without coordination. This is the basis of multi-stage cluster sampling with stratification at each stage.

deepDiveIntro: The population is divided into non-overlapping strata. A separate simple random sample is drawn from each stratum, and the overall estimate is a weighted average of the within-stratum estimates, where the weights are the stratum population sizes divided by the total. This estimator is unbiased under simple random sampling without replacement within each stratum. Its variance depends on within-stratum variability and the sampling fraction in each stratum.

Proportional allocation assigns sample sizes proportional to stratum population sizes — every unit has the same probability of selection. Neyman optimal allocation instead assigns more sampling effort to strata that are larger or more variable, minimizing overall variance for a fixed total sample size. When all strata have the same within-stratum variability, Neyman allocation reduces to proportional.

deepDive.heading: Stratified Estimator and Variance
deepDive.body: The stratified mean combines separate within-stratum averages using population weights. Its main advantage is that it removes between-stratum variation from the estimator, which can reduce variance substantially when the strata are meaningfully different from one another. Stratification therefore buys precision by organizing the population before sampling.
deepDive.formula: Var(x̄_st) = Σ wₕ² · σₕ²(1 − fₕ) / nₕ

deepDive.heading: Proportional vs Neyman Allocation
deepDive.body: Allocation determines how the total sample is spread across strata. Proportional allocation is simple and preserves representation, while Neyman allocation puts more effort into strata that are larger or more variable in order to maximize precision. The best choice depends on whether representativeness, subgroup precision, or overall efficiency is the main goal.

deepDive.heading: Post-Stratification
deepDive.body: Post-stratification uses known population composition after data collection to reweight the sample into better alignment with the target population. It can reduce both bias and variance when response rates differ across important groups. The method is especially useful when full design-stage stratification was not possible but good auxiliary totals are available.

deepDive.heading: Optimal Stratification: Choosing Strata Boundaries
deepDive.body: Good strata are internally homogeneous and externally different. In practice, that means choosing boundaries so that units inside a stratum resemble one another on variables related to the outcome. Beyond a modest number of strata, the extra precision gains often flatten out, so design simplicity starts to matter more.

takeaways: Stratified mean: x̄_st = Σwₕx̄ₕ; unbiased under SRSWOR within strata. [1]
Variance reduction = between-strata variance removed; proportional allocation: Var_prop vs Var_SRS. [1]
Neyman allocation: nₕ ∝ Nₕσₕ; minimizes variance for fixed n. [1]
Disproportionate stratification + HT weighting ensures subgroup precision without biasing overall estimates. [1]
Post-stratification applies stratum weights after collection to reduce non-response bias and improve precision. [1]

edgeCases.q: What if a stratum has nₕ = 1?
edgeCases.a: With nₕ = 1, the within-stratum variance sₕ² is undefined (no degrees of freedom). This 'collapsed stratum' problem can be handled by: (1) collapsing stratum h with an adjacent stratum for variance estimation; (2) using the combined variance across merged strata; (3) using paired selection (nₕ = 2 per stratum, then sₕ² from the pair). Take-Neyman allocation may assign nₕ = 1 to very small strata — ensure a minimum nₕ ≥ 2 in the optimization. [1]

edgeCases.q: How does stratification interact with cluster sampling?
edgeCases.a: Stratified cluster sampling: divide PSUs (primary sampling units, e.g., schools) into strata (by region, school type), then sample clusters within strata using SRS. Within selected clusters, sample units (students). This is the standard design for large-scale surveys (PISA, NHANES): stratification reduces variance; clustering reduces operational cost. Variance estimation requires accounting for both stratification and clustering — typically done with Taylor linearization or jackknife replication. [1]

sources.label: Lohr — Sampling: Design and Analysis (2nd ed.), Ch. 3
